content.problemStatement:
You have an array AAA of length NNN.

You can perform the following operation on it as many times as you like (possibly, zero):


Choose two integers LLL and RRR (1≤L<R≤∣A∣1 \leq L \lt R \leq |A|1≤L<R≤∣A∣);
Delete the elements AL,AL+1,…,ARA_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_RAL​,AL+1​,…,AR​ from AAA; and
Insert the median†^\dagger† of [AL,AL+1,…,AR][A_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_R][AL​,AL+1​,…,AR​] in their place.
Here, ∣A∣|A|∣A∣ denotes the length of AAA. Note that this length reduces by R−LR-LR−L after performing the operation.

For example, if A=[4,2,7,5,9,4]A = [4, 2, 7, 5, 9, 4]A=[4,2,7,5,9,4], choosing L=2L = 2L=2 and R=4R = 4R=4 will result in A=[4,5,9,4]A = [4, 5, 9, 4]A=[4,5,9,4] - the elements 2,7,52, 7, 52,7,5 are deleted, the the median of [2,7,5][2, 7, 5][2,7,5] (which equals 555) is inserted in their place.

For each i=1,2,3,…,Ni = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, Ni=1,2,3,…,N, decide whether there exists a sequence of operations such that after performing this sequence, the resulting array is of length 111 and contains only the single element AiA_iAi​.

†^\dagger†The median of an array BBB of length MMM is the element at index ⌈M2⌉\left\lceil \frac{M}{2} \right\rceil⌈2M​⌉ in the array obtained by sorting BBB in ascending order.
Specifically,


If MMM is odd, the median is the middle element of the sorted array.

For example, if B=[3,1,5,2,6]B = [3, 1, 5, 2, 6]B=[3,1,5,2,6], the sorted array is [1,2,3,5,6][1, 2, 3, 5, 6][1,2,3,5,6], and the median is 333.


If MMM is even, the median is the left of the two middle elements.

For example, if B=[3,1,5,2]B = [3, 1, 5, 2]B=[3,1,5,2], the sorted array is [1,2,3,5][1, 2, 3, 5][1,2,3,5], and the median is 222.



Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the number of elements in AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{N}A1​,A2​,…,AN​, denoting the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, print a binary string SSS of length NNN, where Si=1S_i = 1Si​=1 if and only if it's possible to perform operations in such a way that the final array contains a single element equal to AiA_iAi​.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 4⋅1054\cdot10^54⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The median of [1,1][1, 1][1,1] is 111, so performing the operation on the full array results in [1][1][1].
Test case 222: We have the following:

To get a final value of 222, replace the entire array with its median.
To get a final value of 111,

First, replace [2,3][2, 3][2,3] with its median to obtain A=[1,2]A = [1, 2]A=[1,2].
Then, replace [1,2][1, 2][1,2] with its median to obtain [1][1][1].


It can be shown that no sequence of operations can make 333 be the last remaining element.
